title: MSF Report: 3,396 Sexual Violence Survivors Treated in Darfur; RSF Using Rape as Weapon of War
detail: Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF) released a report on March 31 documenting 3,396 survivors of sexual violence who sought treatment at MSF facilities in North and South Darfur between January 2024 and November 2025 — a figure MSF called 'only a fraction of the true scale.' Of those treated, 97% were women and girls. Survivors 'frequently and clearly' identified RSF fighters as perpetrators. MSF stated this constituted the systematic use of sexual violence as a 'weapon of war' to control civilian populations and constituted a grave violation of international humanitarian law. MSF noted that barriers to reporting — stigma, fear, geographic inaccessibility, and lack of healthcare — meant real numbers were vastly higher than documented. The report called on the international community and ICC to take urgent action to protect civilians and hold perpetrators accountable.

media.caption: MSF report documents 3,396 survivors of sexual violence treated in North and South Darfur facilities between 2024-2025
Women and girls displaced by the conflict in Darfur seek medical care at an MSF facility

title: SAF Reports Repelling Fresh RSF-SPLM-N Attack in Blue Nile; Fighting Continues Near Geissan
detail: The Sudanese Armed Forces issued a statement on March 31 claiming it had repelled a fresh RSF-SPLM-N attack in the Blue Nile region, without providing specific geographic details. The claim, posted through official SAF channels, said forces 'destroyed enemy positions and inflicted significant losses.' The claim could not be independently verified. Separately, fighting continued in the southern Blue Nile zone near Geissan, as RSF-SPLM-N forces maintained pressure on the district approximately one week after the capture of Kurmuk (March 23). The humanitarian situation in Geissan remained critical with ongoing civilian displacement toward Roseires and Ed Damazin. The conflict in Blue Nile — a region that had remained mostly outside the main fronts of the war — had escalated sharply since mid-March 2026, with the RSF-SPLM-N alliance now threatening a third state capital (Ed Damazin) after Khartoum and El Fasher.

media.caption: SAF reports repelling fresh RSF-SPLM-N attack in Blue Nile

title: UN World News Brief: 'Deadly Drone Attacks' Underscore Sudan Civilian Crisis
detail: The United Nations released a World News in Brief bulletin on March 31 highlighting Sudan's escalating drone attacks on civilian targets as a critical humanitarian concern. The brief reiterated the UN's call on both SAF and RSF to immediately cease attacks on civilian populations and facilitate unhindered humanitarian access, particularly to Dilling (South Kordofan), Geissan (Blue Nile), and RSF-held Darfur. The UN noted the UNSC press statement condemning 'repeated drone attacks on civilians and civilian infrastructure' and called for accountability for attacks that may constitute war crimes. Humanitarian agencies reported continued inability to reach Dilling as of March 31 due to the Habila road blockade, with residents relying on dwindling food stocks.

media.caption: UN World News: deadly drone attacks underscore Sudan civilian crisis
